questionTitle: A train running at 60 km/hr crosses a pole in 9 seconds. What is the length of the train?
A boat travels upstream at 10 km/hr and downstream at 18 km/hr. What is the speed of the boat in still water?
In how many ways can the letters of the word 'LEADING' be arranged so that the vowels always come together?
20 men can build a wall in 6 days. How many men are needed to build it in 4 days?
The LCM of 8, 12, 15 and 20 is 120. What is the least number which when divided by each leaves remainder 5?
A sum of money doubles itself in 8 years at simple interest. In how many years will it treble itself?
Pipes A and B can fill a tank in 20 and 30 minutes respectively. Both pipes are opened together. After how many minutes should B be closed so the tank fills in exactly 18 minutes?
A shopkeeper marks an article 40% above cost price and gives a 25% discount. What is his profit percentage?
The average of five consecutive integers starting with 'm' is n. What is the average of five consecutive integers starting with (m+2)?
Find the next term: 2, 6, 12, 20, 30, ?
Two trains of lengths 120m and 80m run on parallel tracks. When running in the same direction at 72 km/hr and 54 km/hr respectively, how long does the faster train take to cross the slower?
A and B can do a job in 12 days, B and C in 15 days, C and A in 20 days. In how many days can all three working together finish the job?
If the ratio of the ages of Raju and Ravi is 3:5 and their combined age is 80, what will be the ratio of their ages after 10 years?
What is the sum of all prime numbers between 1 and 30?
If the ratio of the first to second is 2:3 and that of the second to the third is 5:8, what is the ratio of the first to the third?
A 280-metre long train crosses a platform 220 metres long in 30 seconds. What is the speed of the train in km/hr?
The cost price of 20 articles is equal to the selling price of 16 articles. What is the gain or loss percentage?
How many three-digit numbers are divisible by 7?
The product of two consecutive even integers is 120. What is the smaller integer?
What is the next term in the series: 5, 10, 20, 40, ...?
A invested Rs. 70,000 and B invested Rs. 30,000 in a business. At year end profit was Rs. 40,000. What is B's share of profit?
If a number is increased by 20% and then decreased by 20%, what is the net percentage change?
A circle has a radius of 7 cm. What is its area? (Use π = 22/7)
The sum of two consecutive odd integers is 56. What is the smaller integer?
In a mixture of 60 litres, the ratio of milk and water is 2:1. How much more water must be added to make the ratio 1:2?
At what rate of simple interest per annum will Rs. 6,000 amount to Rs. 8,400 in 4 years?
What is the average of the first 20 odd natural numbers?
Two numbers are in the ratio 3:5. If each is increased by 10, the new ratio is 5:7. Find the original numbers.
What is the sum of the first 30 natural numbers?
A man walking at 5 km/hr reaches his office 6 minutes late. Walking at 6 km/hr he reaches 2 minutes early. What is the distance to his office?
A cistern is filled by pipes A and B in 12 and 15 minutes respectively. Pipe C can empty it in 10 minutes. If all three pipes are opened simultaneously, in how many minutes will the cistern be full?
What is the compound interest on Rs. 8,000 for 2 years at 10% per annum, compounded annually?
Which header provides I/O stream functionality in modern C++ (no .h extension)?
What does the 'cout' object in C++ do?
What is the size of an 'int' on a typical 64-bit system in C++?
What is the difference between 'delete' and 'delete[]' in C++?
options: 'delete' frees a single object; 'delete[]' frees an array
'delete[]' frees a single object; 'delete' frees an array
answer: 'delete' frees a single object; 'delete[]' frees an array

questionTitle: What does the 'new' operator return when memory allocation fails in C++ (without std::nothrow)?
What does 'static' mean when applied to a local variable inside a function in C++?
options: The variable is shared across all instances of the class
The variable retains its value between function calls
answer: The variable retains its value between function calls

questionTitle: A pure virtual function in C++ is declared by assigning it to:
What is the purpose of the 'this' pointer in C++?
options: Points to the previous element in a linked list
Points to the current instance of a class
Points to the next element in a linked list
answer: Points to the current instance of a class

questionTitle: What does the 'const' keyword mean when placed after a member function declaration: `void show() const;`?
options: The function returns a constant value
The function does not modify the object's state
answer: The function does not modify the object's state

questionTitle: What is the difference between 'struct' and 'class' in C++?
options: struct members are public by default; class members are private by default
struct cannot have member functions; class can
answer: struct members are public by default; class members are private by default

questionTitle: Which C++11 feature allows you to deduce the type of a variable from its initialiser?
Which of the following is NOT a valid C++ data type?
What does 'endl' do differently from '\n' in C++?
options: 'endl' inserts a newline AND flushes the buffer; '\n' only inserts a newline
'endl' inserts a tab; '\n' inserts a newline
answer: 'endl' inserts a newline AND flushes the buffer; '\n' only inserts a newline

questionTitle: What is a move constructor in C++11?
options: A constructor that copies resources from another object
A constructor that transfers ownership of resources from a temporary object
A constructor that deletes another object
answer: A constructor that transfers ownership of resources from a temporary object

questionTitle: In C++, what does the 'explicit' keyword on a constructor prevent?
options: Implicit type conversions and copy-initialisation using that constructor
answer: Implicit type conversions and copy-initialisation using that constructor

questionTitle: What does the 'new' operator do in C++?
options: Allocates memory on the heap and returns a pointer to it
Allocates memory on the stack and returns a pointer to it
answer: Allocates memory on the heap and returns a pointer to it

questionTitle: What happens if you do not define a virtual destructor in a base class in C++?
options: The derived class destructor is never called when deleting via base pointer, causing resource leaks
answer: The derived class destructor is never called when deleting via base pointer, causing resource leaks

questionTitle: Which C++11 smart pointer transfers sole ownership and cannot be copied?
What is a lambda expression in C++11?
options: A named function stored in a variable
An anonymous inline function that can capture variables from its enclosing scope
A pointer to a member function
answer: An anonymous inline function that can capture variables from its enclosing scope

questionTitle: What is the output of: `cout << (10 + 20) << " " << (10 + 20);`?
Which of the following is NOT a valid C++ identifier?
What does 'nullptr' represent in C++11 and why was it introduced?
options: An integer 0; introduced as an alias
A type-safe null pointer constant; introduced to avoid ambiguity with integer 0
answer: A type-safe null pointer constant; introduced to avoid ambiguity with integer 0

questionTitle: What is the Rule of Five in C++11?
options: If you define any of: destructor, copy constructor, copy assignment, move constructor, or move assignment, you should define all five
A class must have at most five constructors
Memory must be freed within five seconds
No class should have more than five data members
answer: If you define any of: destructor, copy constructor, copy assignment, move constructor, or move assignment, you should define all five

questionTitle: What is the output of `cout << sizeof(int*);` on a 64-bit system?
What is RAII in C++?
options: Resource Acquisition Is Initialisation — resources are tied to object lifetime
answer: Resource Acquisition Is Initialisation — resources are tied to object lifetime

questionTitle: What does the 'static' keyword do when applied to a class member in C++?
options: Specifies that the member is shared among all instances of the class
Specifies that the member belongs to a specific instance of the class
Specifies that the member cannot be modified
Specifies that the member cannot be accessed from outside its class
answer: Specifies that the member is shared among all instances of the class

questionTitle: What is 'typeid' used for in C++?
options: To identify the type of an object at runtime (RTTI)
To identify the type of an object at compile time
To identify the type of a class
To cast a pointer to another type
answer: To identify the type of an object at runtime (RTTI)

questionTitle: What is template specialisation in C++?
options: Providing a specific implementation of a template for a particular data type
Creating multiple templates with the same name
Restricting a template to a single type
answer: Providing a specific implementation of a template for a particular data type

questionTitle: In C++17, what does 'if constexpr' allow you to do?
options: Evaluate an if-condition at compile time and discard the unused branch
Declare a constant inside an if statement
Use constexpr functions in runtime if-blocks
Nothing new; it is identical to a regular if
answer: Evaluate an if-condition at compile time and discard the unused branch

questionTitle: What does CSS stand for?
Which CSS property changes the text color of an element?
In the CSS box model, what does 'padding' define?
options: Space between the element's content and its border
Space between the element's border and neighbouring elements
Space between the border and the viewport
answer: Space between the element's content and its border

questionTitle: Which CSS property sets the background color of an element?
In the CSS box model, 'margin' defines the space:
options: Between the content and its border
Outside the border — between the element and adjacent elements
answer: Outside the border — between the element and adjacent elements

questionTitle: Which CSS value for 'position' removes an element from the normal document flow and positions it relative to its nearest positioned ancestor?
Which CSS property adds a shadow behind an element's box?
What is the default value of 'flex-direction' in a flex container?
Which value of 'position' keeps an element fixed in the viewport even when the page is scrolled?
What does the CSS property 'z-index' control?
options: The stacking order of positioned elements along the z-axis
The order of elements along the x-axis
The zoom level of the page
answer: The stacking order of positioned elements along the z-axis

questionTitle: What does 'overflow: hidden' do to content that exceeds an element's box?
options: Clips the overflowing content so it is not visible
Wraps it to the next line
answer: Clips the overflowing content so it is not visible

questionTitle: Which CSS property creates rounded corners?
What is the CSS 'transform' property used for?
options: Changing the colour of an element
Rotating, scaling, skewing, or translating an element
Changing the font of an element
answer: Rotating, scaling, skewing, or translating an element

questionTitle: In CSS Grid, which property defines the column widths of a grid container?
Which CSS property changes the font size of an element?
What is the difference between 'visibility: hidden' and 'display: none' in CSS?
options: 'visibility: hidden' hides the element but keeps its space; 'display: none' removes it from flow entirely
'display: none' keeps the space; 'visibility: hidden' removes it from flow
Only 'display: none' works in modern browsers
answer: 'visibility: hidden' hides the element but keeps its space; 'display: none' removes it from flow entirely

questionTitle: Which CSS unit is relative to the font-size of the root element?
Which CSS selector targets an element only when the user hovers over it?
What does 'text-align: justify' do?
options: Stretches lines so that each line has equal width, aligned to both left and right margins
answer: Stretches lines so that each line has equal width, aligned to both left and right margins

questionTitle: Which CSS property controls the spacing between individual letters?
Which flexbox property aligns flex items along the cross-axis?
What does 'calc()' allow you to do in CSS?
options: Call a JavaScript function from CSS
Perform mathematical calculations mixing different units (e.g. 100% - 40px)
Calculate the specificity of a selector
Iterate over a list of values
answer: Perform mathematical calculations mixing different units (e.g. 100% - 40px)

questionTitle: Which CSS at-rule is used to apply styles only when specific conditions (like screen width) are met?
What does 'opacity: 0' do compared to 'visibility: hidden'?
options: 'opacity: 0' makes the element invisible but it still receives pointer events; 'visibility: hidden' does not
'visibility: hidden' makes the element invisible but it can still receive pointer events
answer: 'opacity: 0' makes the element invisible but it still receives pointer events; 'visibility: hidden' does not

questionTitle: In CSS specificity, which selector has the highest specificity weight?
What does 'display: none' do in CSS?
options: Hides the element and removes it from the document flow
Displays the element as a block
answer: Hides the element and removes it from the document flow

questionTitle: What is a CSS custom property (CSS variable) and how is it declared?
options: A variable declared with 'var keyword' in CSS
A property prefixed with '--' (e.g. '--primary-color: blue;') and used via var()
A JavaScript variable accessible in CSS
answer: A property prefixed with '--' (e.g. '--primary-color: blue;') and used via var()

questionTitle: Which CSS property controls how an element handles long words that overflow their container?
What does 'font-weight: 700' render as?
What does 'line-height: 1.5' mean when applied to a paragraph with a 16px font size?
What does the CSS 'clip-path' property do?
options: Defines a clipping region, hiding parts of an element outside the region
Clips an image to a circle
Applies a shadow to an element
answer: Defines a clipping region, hiding parts of an element outside the region

questionTitle: In a CSS Grid layout, what does 'fr' unit represent?
options: A fractional unit of the available free space in the grid container
answer: A fractional unit of the available free space in the grid container

questionTitle: What is the plural form of 'child'?
Which word is a synonym for 'happy'?
What is the past tense of 'go'?
Which sentence is grammatically correct?
options: She do not like ice cream.

questionTitle: What is the antonym of 'beautiful'?
Choose the word closest in meaning to 'EPHEMERAL'.
What is the superlative form of 'good'?
Choose the correct spelling.
Which sentence is grammatically correct?
options: Neither of them are coming to the party.
Neither of them is coming to the party.
Neither of them am coming to the party.
Neither of them were coming to the party.
answer: Neither of them is coming to the party.

questionTitle: Identify the antonym of 'generous'.
What is the correct plural form of 'criterion'?
Select the appropriate preposition: She is good _____ mathematics.

Which sentence correctly uses a dangling modifier?
(Identify the INCORRECT sentence — it has the dangling modifier.)
options: Walking down the street, the trees looked beautiful.
Having finished the exam, she left the hall.
After reading the novel, she found it inspiring.
Exhausted from the journey, he went straight to bed.
answer: Walking down the street, the trees looked beautiful.

questionTitle: Identify the correct sentence.
options: Each of the students were given a book.
Each of the students was given a book.
Each of the students are given a book.
Each of the students have been given a book.
answer: Each of the students was given a book.

questionTitle: Identify the correct use of the gerund:
Choose the sentence that correctly uses a semicolon:
options: I went to the store; and bought milk.
She studied hard; she passed the exam.
He likes pizza; but not pasta.
They went home; because it rained.
answer: She studied hard; she passed the exam.

questionTitle: Identify the correct use of the infinitive:
options: He wanted to to go home.

questionTitle: Select the correct form: 'She _____ English for five years.'
Which sentence contains a gerund used as the subject?
options: She enjoys to swim in the ocean.
Swimming in the ocean is her favourite activity.
She likes to swim in the ocean.
She went to swim in the ocean.
answer: Swimming in the ocean is her favourite activity.

questionTitle: Choose the word that best fills the blank: 'The politician's speech was so _____ that the crowd burst into applause.'
Which of the following words is a synonym for 'confused'?
What is the passive voice of: 'The committee approved the proposal'?
options: The proposal was approving by the committee.
The proposal was approved by the committee.
The proposal is approved by the committee.
The proposal had been approved by committee.
answer: The proposal was approved by the committee.

questionTitle: Identify the correct use of the past perfect tense:
options: She had wrote the letter before she left.
She had written the letter before she left.
She had writing the letter before she left.
She had writed the letter before she left.
answer: She had written the letter before she left.

questionTitle: Choose the word that is OPPOSITE in meaning to 'LOQUACIOUS'.
Identify the correct use of the subjunctive mood:
options: If she was here, she would help.
If she were here, she would help.
If she will be here, she would help.
If she is here, she would help.
answer: If she were here, she would help.

questionTitle: Identify the figure of speech in: 'The classroom was a zoo.'
Choose the correct sentence:
options: A fewer number of people was present.

questionTitle: The word 'ubiquitous' most nearly means:
Identify the correct use of the past perfect continuous tense:
options: She had been crying when I arrived.
She had cried when I arrived.
She had been cry when I arrived.
She had crying when I arrived.
answer: She had been crying when I arrived.

questionTitle: Select the correct form: 'He _____ for hours before he found it.'
Which sentence contains a split infinitive?
options: He promised to always keep his word.
They wanted to finish the project.
She hoped to win the prize.
answer: He promised to always keep his word.

questionTitle: What is the meaning of the idiom 'bite the bullet'?
options: To lose courage at a critical moment
To endure a painful or difficult situation with courage
To speak bluntly without regard for others
answer: To endure a painful or difficult situation with courage

questionTitle: Which planet is known as the 'Red Planet'?
What is the capital city of Japan?
Who wrote the play 'Romeo and Juliet'?
In which year did the Titanic sink?

What is the chemical symbol for gold?
Who is known as the 'God of Cricket'?
Which country won the first ICC Cricket World Cup in 1975?
Who holds the record for the highest individual score (501*) in first-class cricket?
Which bowler has taken the most wickets in international cricket (all formats combined)?
In international cricket (all formats), who has hit the most sixes as of 2024?
Who was the captain of the Indian cricket team when they won the 2011 ICC Cricket World Cup?
India's Chandrayaan-3 successfully landed near the lunar south pole in which year?
Which country has won the most ICC Cricket World Cups (50-over)?
How many justices (including the Chief Justice) sit on the Supreme Court of India?
Which Indian bowler overtook Anil Kumble to become India's highest Test wicket-taker?
Who is known as the 'Missile Man of India'?
What is the national animal of India?

What does HTML stand for?
Which HTML tag defines an unordered list?
What is the correct HTML element for inserting a line break?
Which HTML attribute provides alternate text for an image for accessibility and broken-image fallback?
Which <meta> tag makes a page responsive on mobile devices?
Which HTML tag creates a hyperlink?
What is the purpose of the DOCTYPE declaration in HTML5?
options: Tells the browser to render the document in standards mode
answer: Tells the browser to render the document in standards mode

questionTitle: What is the correct HTML5 tag for embedding a video?
What semantic meaning does the <footer> element convey?
options: Closing or bottom-of-section content such as copyright, links, or authorship
answer: Closing or bottom-of-section content such as copyright, links, or authorship

questionTitle: Which attribute specifies the URL the hyperlink points to?
What is the difference between <b> and <strong> in HTML5?
options: <b> is for visual boldness only; <strong> carries semantic importance
<b> is deprecated and should never be used
answer: <b> is for visual boldness only; <strong> carries semantic importance

questionTitle: Which HTML element should be used for the main content that is unique to a page?
Which attribute makes a form field mandatory before submission?
What does the <nav> tag represent?
Which HTML tag defines a table row?
What does the 'alt' attribute of the tag provide?
options: Alternate text for accessibility and when image cannot be displayed
answer: Alternate text for accessibility and when image cannot be displayed

questionTitle: What is the purpose of the 'data-*' attributes in HTML5?
options: To store custom data privately to the page or application
answer: To store custom data privately to the page or application

questionTitle: Which HTML5 input type validates that the user enters a properly formatted email address?
What is the correct HTML for creating an email link?
Which attribute on specifies the image URL?
What is the difference between <section> and <div> in HTML5?
options: <section> has semantic meaning representing a thematic grouping; <div> is a generic non-semantic container
<div> is for layout only and is deprecated in HTML5
answer: <section> has semantic meaning representing a thematic grouping; <div> is a generic non-semantic container

questionTitle: What does the <fieldset> tag do?
options: Groups related form controls together with an optional caption
answer: Groups related form controls together with an optional caption

questionTitle: Which HTML5 element is used to play audio files natively in the browser?
Which HTML attribute opens a link in a new browser tab?
Which security attribute should accompany target='_blank' to prevent tab-napping attacks?
What is the purpose of the `canvas` element in HTML5?
options: To define a section of content
To draw graphics or animations programmatically via JavaScript
answer: To draw graphics or animations programmatically via JavaScript

questionTitle: What does the 'contenteditable' attribute do?
options: Specifies whether the element's content is editable by the user
answer: Specifies whether the element's content is editable by the user

questionTitle: What does the HTML <template> element do?
options: Defines a reusable HTML snippet that is not rendered until activated by JavaScript
Acts as a placeholder for content
answer: Defines a reusable HTML snippet that is not rendered until activated by JavaScript

questionTitle: What is the purpose of the <details> and <summary> tags in HTML5?
options: To create a collapsible section where <summary> is the visible heading
answer: To create a collapsible section where <summary> is the visible heading

questionTitle: Which HTML attribute enables native browser spell checking on an input or editable element?
What does ARIA stand for and what is its purpose in HTML?
options: Automated Rendering Interface API — speeds up page rendering
Accessible Rich Internet Applications — adds accessibility semantics for assistive technologies
Advanced Responsive Image Attributes — optimises images
Async Resource Integration API — manages resource loading
answer: Accessible Rich Internet Applications — adds accessibility semantics for assistive technologies

questionTitle: Which HTML5 element provides a way to embed another HTML page within the current page in an isolated context?
Java achieves platform independence through which mechanism?
options: Compiling to native machine code for each OS
Interpreting source code directly each time
Compiling to bytecode that runs on the JVM on any platform
Using a shared C runtime library
answer: Compiling to bytecode that runs on the JVM on any platform

questionTitle: Which keyword is used to define a constant in Java?
What is the difference between an interface and an abstract class in Java?
options: An interface can have method implementations; an abstract class cannot
An abstract class can have state (fields) and concrete methods; an interface traditionally defines only a contract (Java 8+ allows default methods)
A class can extend multiple abstract classes but implement only one interface
answer: An abstract class can have state (fields) and concrete methods; an interface traditionally defines only a contract (Java 8+ allows default methods)

questionTitle: What is the Java Virtual Machine (JVM)?
options: A physical machine used to run Java code
An abstract machine that provides the runtime environment for Java bytecode
answer: An abstract machine that provides the runtime environment for Java bytecode

questionTitle: Which Java data type stores a single Unicode character?
What is the purpose of the 'static' keyword applied to a method in Java?
options: The method belongs to the class itself, not to instances, and can be called without creating an object
To declare a method that can be overridden
answer: The method belongs to the class itself, not to instances, and can be called without creating an object

questionTitle: What is the default value of a boolean field in a Java class?
What does 'void' as a return type indicate in Java?
options: The method returns an empty string
The method does not return any value
answer: The method does not return any value

questionTitle: Which of the following is NOT a Java keyword?
What is the key difference between '==' and '.equals()' when comparing String objects in Java?
options: '==' compares object references (memory addresses); '.equals()' compares the actual string content
answer: '==' compares object references (memory addresses); '.equals()' compares the actual string content

questionTitle: What is the Java String Pool?
options: A data structure for managing char arrays
An area in heap memory where String literals are cached and reused to save memory
A thread-safe queue for string processing
The maximum size of a String object
answer: An area in heap memory where String literals are cached and reused to save memory

questionTitle: What is method overriding in Java?
options: Defining multiple methods with the same name but different parameters
Providing a specific implementation of an inherited method in a subclass with the same signature
Allowing a method to return multiple types
Calling a method before the object is fully constructed
answer: Providing a specific implementation of an inherited method in a subclass with the same signature

questionTitle: What does the 'super' keyword allow you to do in Java?
options: Call the current class's constructor only
Access the superclass's constructor, methods, and fields from the subclass
Create an instance of the parent class
answer: Access the superclass's constructor, methods, and fields from the subclass

questionTitle: What is the purpose of the 'this' keyword in Java?
options: To call the constructor of the current class
To refer to the current instance of the class
answer: To refer to the current instance of the class

questionTitle: What does 'checked exception' mean in Java?
options: An exception that is checked at runtime
An exception that must be either caught or declared in the method signature using 'throws'
An exception thrown by the JVM only
An exception caused by programmer error that should not be caught
answer: An exception that must be either caught or declared in the method signature using 'throws'

questionTitle: What is the purpose of the 'interface' keyword in Java?
options: To define a contract (abstract type) that classes can implement
answer: To define a contract (abstract type) that classes can implement

questionTitle: What is the purpose of the 'try-with-resources' statement introduced in Java 7?
options: To retry a block of code on failure
To automatically close resources (like streams) that implement AutoCloseable when the try block exits
To handle multiple exceptions in one catch block
answer: To automatically close resources (like streams) that implement AutoCloseable when the try block exits

questionTitle: What is method overloading in Java?
options: Defining multiple methods with the same name but different parameters in the same class
Defining multiple methods with the same name and same parameters
Overriding a method in a subclass
Defining multiple methods in different classes
answer: Defining multiple methods with the same name but different parameters in the same class

questionTitle: What is the difference between ArrayList and LinkedList in Java?
options: ArrayList uses a dynamic array (fast random access, slow insertions in middle); LinkedList uses doubly-linked nodes (fast insertions, slow random access)
LinkedList supports random access; ArrayList does not
ArrayList is thread-safe; LinkedList is not
answer: ArrayList uses a dynamic array (fast random access, slow insertions in middle); LinkedList uses doubly-linked nodes (fast insertions, slow random access)

questionTitle: What does the 'volatile' keyword guarantee in Java multithreading?
options: Only one thread can access the variable at a time
The variable is never cached; reads and writes go directly to main memory, ensuring visibility across threads
The variable is immutable after creation
The variable is deleted after the thread ends
answer: The variable is never cached; reads and writes go directly to main memory, ensuring visibility across threads

questionTitle: What does the Java Stream API's 'collect(Collectors.toList())' operation do?
options: Terminates the stream and gathers all elements into a List
Counts the elements in the stream
answer: Terminates the stream and gathers all elements into a List

questionTitle: What is the purpose of the 'finally' block in Java?
options: Executes only when no exception is thrown
Executes only when an exception is thrown
Always executes after try/catch regardless of whether an exception occurred
Declares a method that cannot be overridden
answer: Always executes after try/catch regardless of whether an exception occurred

questionTitle: What is a functional interface in Java 8?
options: An interface with exactly one abstract method, enabling lambda expressions
An interface with only static methods
answer: An interface with exactly one abstract method, enabling lambda expressions

questionTitle: What is the difference between HashMap and ConcurrentHashMap in Java?
options: HashMap is not thread-safe; ConcurrentHashMap allows concurrent reads and segment-level writes safely
ConcurrentHashMap is slower in all cases
HashMap uses a tree structure; ConcurrentHashMap uses a linked list
answer: HashMap is not thread-safe; ConcurrentHashMap allows concurrent reads and segment-level writes safely

questionTitle: What is garbage collection in Java?
options: The JVM automatically reclaims memory used by objects that are no longer reachable
Deleting unused classes at compile time
answer: The JVM automatically reclaims memory used by objects that are no longer reachable

questionTitle: What is the 'Optional' class in Java 8 designed to solve?
options: Replace all null checks with exceptions
Wrap a value that may or may not be present, reducing NullPointerExceptions
answer: Wrap a value that may or may not be present, reducing NullPointerExceptions

questionTitle: What does the @Override annotation do in Java?
options: Marks a method to be executed in a new thread
Instructs the compiler to verify the method actually overrides a superclass method, causing a compile error if it does not
Prevents a method from being overridden
answer: Instructs the compiler to verify the method actually overrides a superclass method, causing a compile error if it does not

questionTitle: What is the result of: `2 + 2 + '2'`?
What is the difference between 'var', 'let', and 'const' in JavaScript?
options: 'var' is function-scoped and hoisted; 'let' and 'const' are block-scoped; 'const' cannot be reassigned
'let' is global; 'var' is block-scoped; 'const' is function-scoped
answer: 'var' is function-scoped and hoisted; 'let' and 'const' are block-scoped; 'const' cannot be reassigned

questionTitle: What does '===' (strict equality) do differently from '==' in JavaScript?
options: Checks both value AND type without type coercion
Assigns a value to a variable
answer: Checks both value AND type without type coercion

questionTitle: Which of the following is NOT a primitive data type in JavaScript?
What does 'typeof null' return in JavaScript, and why is it considered a bug?
options: 'object' — a historical bug from the original implementation
answer: 'object' — a historical bug from the original implementation

questionTitle: Which array method adds one or more elements to the end and returns the new length?
What will `5 == '5'` return in JavaScript and why?
options: true — '==' coerces the string '5' to the number 5 before comparing
answer: true — '==' coerces the string '5' to the number 5 before comparing

questionTitle: What is event delegation in JavaScript?
options: Attaching a separate event listener to every child element
Attaching a single event listener to a parent element to handle events bubbling up from children
Delegating events to the browser's default handler
answer: Attaching a single event listener to a parent element to handle events bubbling up from children

questionTitle: What does Array.prototype.map() do?
options: Filters elements that pass a test
Returns a new array with each element transformed by the provided function
Modifies the original array in place
answer: Returns a new array with each element transformed by the provided function

questionTitle: What is a Promise in JavaScript?
options: A synchronous function that blocks execution
An object representing the eventual completion or failure of an asynchronous operation
answer: An object representing the eventual completion or failure of an asynchronous operation

questionTitle: Which keyword creates an arrow function in JavaScript?
What is the output of `console.log(typeof undefined)`?
What is a closure in JavaScript?
options: A function that closes the browser tab
A function that has access to variables from its enclosing scope even after the outer function has returned
A way to close event listeners
answer: A function that has access to variables from its enclosing scope even after the outer function has returned

questionTitle: What does Array.prototype.filter() return?
options: The first element that passes the test
A new array containing only elements for which the callback returned true
The number of elements that pass the test
It modifies the original array in place
answer: A new array containing only elements for which the callback returned true

questionTitle: What does Array.prototype.reduce() do?
options: Returns a new array with modified elements
Applies a function against an accumulator and each element to produce a single cumulative value
answer: Applies a function against an accumulator and each element to produce a single cumulative value

questionTitle: What is the output of `console.log(3 * '2')`?
What does the 'async/await' syntax in JavaScript do?
options: Provides cleaner syntax for working with Promises, making asynchronous code look synchronous
answer: Provides cleaner syntax for working with Promises, making asynchronous code look synchronous

questionTitle: What does Function.prototype.bind() do?
options: Calls the function immediately with a given 'this'
Returns a new function with 'this' permanently bound to the specified object
Removes a function from the event queue
Creates a copy of the function's arguments
answer: Returns a new function with 'this' permanently bound to the specified object

questionTitle: What is the JavaScript event loop?
options: A loop that attaches event listeners
A mechanism that allows JS (single-threaded) to perform non-blocking operations by offloading tasks and picking up callbacks from the task queue
A for-loop that iterates over DOM events
answer: A mechanism that allows JS (single-threaded) to perform non-blocking operations by offloading tasks and picking up callbacks from the task queue

questionTitle: What is the output of `console.log(typeof NaN)`?
What is the difference between call() and apply() in JavaScript?
options: call() accepts arguments individually; apply() accepts arguments as an array
apply() permanently binds 'this'; call() does not
call() works only on arrays; apply() works on objects
answer: call() accepts arguments individually; apply() accepts arguments as an array

questionTitle: What does `console.log(0.1 + 0.2 === 0.3)` output and why?
options: false — due to IEEE 754 floating-point precision errors, 0.1 + 0.2 is slightly more than 0.3
answer: false — due to IEEE 754 floating-point precision errors, 0.1 + 0.2 is slightly more than 0.3

questionTitle: What does the spread operator (...) do in JavaScript?
options: Creates a deep clone of an object
Expands an iterable (array, string) or object properties into individual elements or key-value pairs
answer: Expands an iterable (array, string) or object properties into individual elements or key-value pairs

questionTitle: What does JavaScript's 'use strict' directive do?
options: Forces all variables to be typed
Enables strict mode: disallows silent errors (e.g. undeclared variables), prevents use of reserved words, and improves performance in some engines
answer: Enables strict mode: disallows silent errors (e.g. undeclared variables), prevents use of reserved words, and improves performance in some engines

questionTitle: What is the output of `console.log(3 + 2 * 5)`?
What does the 'optional chaining' operator (?.) do in JavaScript (ES2020)?
options: Safely accesses a nested property, returning undefined instead of throwing an error if an intermediate value is null/undefined
answer: Safely accesses a nested property, returning undefined instead of throwing an error if an intermediate value is null/undefined

questionTitle: Which of the following is a falsy value in JavaScript?
What is the output of `console.log('2' + 2)`?
What is the 'nullish coalescing' operator (??) in JavaScript and when does it return the right-hand side?
options: Returns the right-hand side when the left is any falsy value
Returns the right-hand side only when the left is null or undefined
Returns the left-hand side when the right is null
It is identical to the || operator
answer: Returns the right-hand side only when the left is null or undefined
